H3588.plain: The most versatile particle in Biblical Hebrew — it can mean 'because' (ground), 'that' (content marker after verbs of knowing/saying), 'when' (temporal), 'although' (concessive), or 'indeed' (emphatic). Context always determines which. Missing this distinction causes significant misreadings: 'The Lord is my shepherd כִּי I shall not want' means 'therefore' or 'so that', not 'because.'

example.note: 'Even though (כִּי) I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil.' Here כִּי is concessive — 'even though / although.' The verse is not giving a reason but acknowledging a reality while asserting courage.

H3651.plain: 'Therefore', 'consequently', 'for this reason.' The standard Hebrew inferential particle, equivalent to Greek οὖν. Marks a conclusion drawn from the preceding argument or narrative. Common at the pivots of prophetic judgment oracles.

example.note: 'לָכֵן the Lord himself will give you a sign.' The therefore links the sign promise to the preceding context of Ahaz's refusal to ask for a sign — the sign comes despite, and because of, his refusal.

H2005.plain: 'Behold', 'look', 'see.' This particle does not just draw attention — it pulls the reader into the immediate experience of the speaker. It is a demonstrative of close psychological presence: 'here, right now, look.' Translations that render it 'behold' often sound archaic; 'look!' or 'just then' often captures the Hebrew better.

example.note: 'And Abraham lifted up his eyes and looked, and הִנֵּה — behind him a ram, caught in a thicket.' The הִנֵּה marks the moment of sudden discovery from Abraham's perspective — the reader sees what Abraham sees at the moment he sees it.

H0408.plain: Prohibitive negation used with the jussive or imperfect — 'do not (continue to).' Similar to Greek μή with present imperative. אַל prohibits an action that is either ongoing or contextually live. It carries an urgent, immediate sense: 'stop this right now' or 'don't you dare.'

example.note: 'אַל תִּשְׁלַח יָדְךָ אֶל-הַנַּעַר' — 'Do not lay your hand on the boy.' The angel uses אַל at the moment of crisis — the most urgent form of prohibition, stopping an action in process.

H3808.plain: Absolute, factual negation — 'not', 'never.' Used with the indicative or when a permanent/general prohibition is in view. The Ten Commandments use לֹא ('You shall not murder') — stating an absolute moral reality, not just forbidding an ongoing action.

example.note: All five commands ('You shall not murder/commit adultery/steal...') use לֹא — absolute prohibitions, not situational ones. The grammar itself supports reading these as unconditional moral laws.

H1571.plain: 'Also', 'even', 'too', 'indeed.' Adds to what was said or intensifies it. When it stacks (גַּם...גַּם), it means 'both...and.' When used before an unexpected subject or action, it signals emphasis: 'even he', 'and what is more.'

example.note: 'גַּם though I walk through the valley...' — often missed in translation. The גַּם signals 'even in this extreme case' — the very worst that could happen is included in the shepherd's protection.

H7535.plain: 'Only', 'but', 'just.' Limits the scope of what was said — either restricting it to a single focus or contrasting with something broader. Often functions as a pastoral pivot: 'do not fear; only do this.'

example.note: 'רַק be strong and very courageous to observe to do all the law...' — Joshua is told to be courageous about one specific thing: Torah obedience. The רַק focuses and sharpens the command.

H0389.plain: Multivalent like כִּי — can mean 'only/certainly' (assurance: 'surely this is true') or 'but/however' (limitation/contrast). Context distinguishes. When emphatic, it functions like 'truly' or 'surely'; when contrastive, like 'but', 'however', 'yet.'

example.note: 'אַךְ אֶל-אֱלֹהִים דּוּמִיָּה נַפְשִׁי' — 'My soul אַךְ (truly/only) rests in God.' The particle intensifies the exclusivity of the rest — God and no other. Some translate 'truly', others 'only.'

H5750.plain: 'Still', 'yet', 'again', 'furthermore', 'any longer.' Temporal or additive. When it means 'still', it emphasizes continuation of a state. 'No longer' (לֹא עוֹד) marks the end of a state. Prophetically, עוֹד often introduces a further revelation: 'and again / and more than this.'

example.note: 'For they shall all know me... and I will remember their sin עוֹד לֹא (no more).' The עוֹד לֹא marks the complete end of the old covenant's sin-and-forgiveness cycle — a permanent state change.

H6258.plain: 'Now' — marks a temporal transition or turning point in the narrative or argument. Often signals that the speaker is moving from background/past to present reality or from premise to application. Can be urgent ('now therefore') or simply transitional ('now at this point').

example.note: 'And עַתָּה, lest he reach out his hand and take also of the tree of life...' — the עַתָּה marks God's immediate response to the new post-fall situation, pivoting from the verdict to the action.

H0518.plain: The standard conditional — 'if'. Less grammatically nuanced than Greek's three-class conditional system, but context (and sometimes the verb form used) distinguishes genuine conditions from rhetorical ones. In oaths, אִם often functions as a strong negative: 'If I do X!' means 'I absolutely will not do X' (implied: may God judge me if I do).

example.note: 'אִם יְבֹאוּן אֶל-מְנוּחָתִי' — in God's oath, this means 'They shall NOT enter my rest.' The אִם in oath context flips to a strong negation. Hebrews 3:11 quotes this and the Greek translator recognizes this: εἰ εἰσελεύσονται — same construction.

H5921.plain: 'Therefore', 'for this reason', 'because of this.' A compound inferential — stronger and more explicit than simple כֵּן alone. Marks a conclusion that is firmly grounded in what preceded. Common at etiological conclusions in Genesis ('therefore to this day...') and prophetic pivots.

example.note: 'עַל-כֵּן a man shall leave his father and his mother...' — the 'therefore' grounds the marriage institution in the creation narrative of Adam and Eve. Jesus cites this עַל-כֵּן in his teaching on divorce (Matt 19:5).

H0199.plain: 'But', 'however', 'yet.' Strong adversative. Less common than אַךְ or רַק. When it appears, it marks a genuine pivot or concession — 'I grant what was said, however...' Often used to introduce the unexpected: after describing human weakness, it pivots to divine strength.

example.note: 'אוּלָם God will ransom my soul from the power of Sheol.' After describing how wealth cannot redeem anyone from death (vv.7-14), the אוּלָם marks the one exception — not wealth, but God.

H0834.plain: The Hebrew relative pronoun — 'who', 'which', 'that.' Unlike Greek relative pronouns, אֲשֶׁר does not decline for gender, number, or case. It simply introduces a relative clause. This undifferentiated form means Hebrew relative clauses are syntactically simpler than Greek ones, but also less precise — the semantic relationship between the clause and its antecedent must be inferred.

example.note: 'Blessed is the man אֲשֶׁר walks not in the counsel of the wicked...' — the אֲשֶׁר introduces three characterizing relative clauses that define the 'blessed man' negatively (vv.1) before the positive definition (v.2).

H7592.plain: An untranslatable particle of entreaty or urging — often rendered 'please', 'I pray', or simply omitted. When someone uses נָא, they are softening a command or request, adding urgency or pathos. It appears at the most emotionally charged moments in the narrative.

example.note: 'Take נָא your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love...' — God's command to Abraham uses נָא, adding a tone of personal, solemn request rather than bare command. The grammar encodes divine sensitivity at the most painful moment in the patriarchal narrative.
